title: Aḍhāra Pāpasthānaka Sūtra — The Eighteen Sources of Sin
summary: This short text names the eighteen 'seats of sin' (pāpa-sthānaka) — the eighteen broad doorways through which a soul takes on harmful karma, from killing, lying, stealing and possessiveness, through the inner passions of anger, pride, deceit and greed, to backbiting, slander and finally wrong belief. After listing all eighteen, the reciter confesses any of them he may have committed himself, had others commit, or merely approved of in others, and asks that all such fault be undone with the words 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' ('may my misdeed come to nothing'). It is recited in the Devasi (daily, sunset) pratikramaṇa as part of preparing the mind for the equanimity vow.
ritualPlacement: In the Devasi pratikramaṇa this sūtra sits in the short preparatory-confession cluster that follows the kāyotsarga and Logassa: Logassa → Gamaṇāgamaṇo (faults of coming and going) → Jīvarāśi (the classes of living beings) → **Aḍhāra Pāpasthānaka** → Dravya-Kṣetra-Kāla-Bhāva, leading into Karemi Bhante (the sāmāyika vow). It thus broadens the reflection from harm done while moving (Iriyāvahiyaṁ/Gamaṇāgamaṇo) to a complete checklist of every category of sin before the practitioner formally takes the vow. Per the inputs it is used in the **devasi** observance; like the other core preparatory sūtras it would recur unchanged (subject only to the standard pakkhī/cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī word-substitution that governs shared sūtras) wherever the same sāmāyika-taking procedure is invoked, and the cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī observances carry no independent text of their own.
authorAndSource: The doctrinal list of eighteen pāpa-sthānaka is canonical and not the work of a single named author; it is attested in the Śvetāmbara Āgamas (e.g. Sthānāṅga and Samavāyāṅga) and systematised in later karma and conduct literature. The recited form here is the traditional medieval Gujarati confessional formula used in the Pratikramaṇa liturgy; no individual composer is named in the source book. ('—' for a specific author.)
structureArc: Being a single-verse sūtra (v.1), its progression is internal rather than across verses. It unfolds in three movements: (a) the **enumeration** — eighteen items named in fixed order, first→eighteenth: prāṇātipāta, mṛṣāvāda, adattādāna, maithuna, parigraha (the five great sins), then the four kaṣāyas krodha, māna, māyā, lobha, then rāga and dveṣa, then kalaha, abhyākhyāna, 'chāḍī' (paiśunya / tale-bearing), rati-arati, para-parivāda, māyā-mṛṣāvāda, and finally mithyātva-śalya; (b) the **confession clause** — for whichever of these 'my soul' has committed (sevyuṁ), had committed by another (sevarāvyuṁ), or approved while another did it (anumodyuṁ); (c) the **sealing** — 'I retract it all by mind, speech and body: micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.' The list deliberately moves from outward acts to inner passions and ends with mithyātva, the root distortion of view from which the rest spring.
recurringImagery: The governing motif is the **triple × triple confession grid** that recurs throughout the Pratikramaṇa. Every fault is owned along two axes at once: the three modes of **agency** — kṛta (doing it oneself, sevyuṁ), kārita (causing another to do it, sevarāvyuṁ), and anumodita (consenting to or applauding another's doing of it, anumodyuṁ); and the three **instruments** or yogas — mana (mind), vacana (speech), kāya (body). Confessing 'by mind, speech and body' across all three modes of agency leaves no loophole through which guilt could be quietly retained. The same grid frames Iriyāvahiyaṁ and the atiyāra confessions, so it is explained here once and need not be re-glossed per item.
keyThemes: ālocanā — frank self-examination before the vow
renunciation of harm by mind, speech and body
the four passions (kaṣāya) as inner roots of sin
rāga–dveṣa as the twin engine of bondage
mithyātva as the deepest, view-level fault
complete confession across doing, causing, approving
micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ — undoing fault by sincere retraction

glossary.definition: A 'seat' or source of sin; one of the eighteen broad categories of conduct through which a soul incurs harmful (pāpa) karma.
Injury to or destruction of life; violence — the first and gravest source of sin.
Taking what has not been given; theft.
Possessiveness; attachment to possessions and accumulation.
The four corrosive passions — krodha (anger), māna (pride), māyā (deceit) and lobha (greed) — items six through nine, which stain the soul and prolong bondage.
Attachment or liking; the pull toward what is pleasant.
Aversion or hatred; the push against what is unpleasant. With rāga it forms the twin root of karmic bondage.
False accusation; imputing a fault to someone that they do not have.
Tale-bearing or backbiting (Gujarati 'chāḍī'); secretly carrying others' faults to cause discord.
Indulgent delight and discontented aversion — taking pleasure in the sinful and displeasure in the wholesome.
Censuring or slandering others; finding fault with people behind their backs.
Deceitful falsehood; lying compounded with cunning intent to mislead.
The 'thorn' (śalya) of wrong belief; deluded or false view of reality — the eighteenth and most fundamental source of sin, since it underlies the others.
Prakrit confessional seal: 'may my misdeed be undone / come to nothing' — the formal retraction that closes the confession.
The three modes of agency — doing a deed oneself (sevyuṁ), causing another to do it (sevarāvyuṁ), and approving another's doing of it (anumodyuṁ) — all of which are confessed.

howToRead: A newcomer can read it as a clear moral checklist: hear the eighteen named, ask honestly whether you have done, prompted, or applauded any of them, and let 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' be a sincere wiping-clean. An advanced practitioner will note the deliberate ordering — outward acts, then the kaṣāyas, then rāga-dveṣa, then social and verbal faults, culminating in mithyātva as the view-level root — and will fold the triple-agency / triple-instrument grid into the confession so that nothing is left unexamined.

sources.title: Jaina Sūtras (Sthānāṅga / Samavāyāṅga context for the eighteen pāpasthānaka)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the canonical list, ordering, and standard English glosses of the eighteen pāpasthānaka.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti), ch. on āsrava and the passions
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal framing of kaṣāya, rāga–dveṣa and mithyātva as causes of karmic influx.

notes: Item 14 appears in the recited Gujarati as 'chāḍī' (tale-bearing), which corresponds to the classical Sanskrit term paiśunya. The verse pairs items 17 (māyā-mṛṣāvāda) and 18 (mithyātva-śalya) as compounds; some enumerations list 'māyā', 'mithyātva' and 'śalya' slightly differently, but the eighteen-fold total and the canonical content are stable across traditions. Author of the specific recited formula is not named in the source book and is left as '—'. The 'soul / my jīva' phrasing ('māre jīve') is the reciter's own soul as the locus of confession.
